assertiveness_why: Harry repeatedly cites outside data and named theses (Paul Graham's 'Don't Talk to Corp Dev' essay, the 'companies are bought not sold' maxim, LinkedIn's Emily Choi's M&A slowdown comment) and directly asks James to agree or push back against them.
question_style: Well-researched and citation-heavy, planting specific outside theses/quotes from other named guests and essays for the guest to respond to or rebut, with real follow-up chains, though he ultimately agrees with most answers rather than contesting them himself.
tone: Professional and enthusiastic, less matey banter than usual, focused squarely on the corp dev/M&A subject matter with minimal personal tangents.
depth: Strong technical/process depth — covers M&A market dynamics, pricing mechanics, internal deal approval workflows, term sheet conversion rates, and post-acquisition integration metrics in granular detail.
harry_quotes: Where would you stand on the thesis that companies are bought and not sold?
How would you respond to Paul Graham's essay, Don't Talk to Corp Dev?
Is it a unanimous voting structure for the execs?
learnings: James rejects the 'bought not sold' maxim, arguing most M&A follows years of deliberate relationship-building between founders/investors and potential acquirers.
He sees the M&A market diversifying beyond the five big tech acquirers toward large non-giant 'unicorns' and non-tech corporates entering the space.
He partially disagrees with Paul Graham's advice to avoid corp dev, arguing building acquirer relationships early is valuable regardless of outcome.
Square's internal process requires early product-team buy-in and an executive conversation before any written term sheet is issued, to avoid reversing course later.
Once a term sheet is signed, deals close at a very high rate industry-wide, but pre-term-sheet likelihood is below 50%.
notable: Rare episode with a non-VC corporate development guest, structured around a hypothetical mock scenario of Square acquiring the 20VC podcast itself.
